Column 2026-04-01

Western Cape Tourism Continues Growth in Q1 2024

South Africa's Western Cape, one of Africa's most attractive tourism destinations, has long been renowned globally for its magnificent natural landscapes, diverse cultural experiences, and world-class wine regions. Cape Town, the provincial capital,

LP
Leo Pan - 潘品樺
  1. 首頁
  3. 專欄
  5. Column

Introduction

South Africa's Western Cape, one of Africa's most attractive tourism destinations, has long been renowned globally for its magnificent natural landscapes, diverse cultural experiences, and world-class wine regions. Cape Town, the provincial capital, is hailed as one of the "world's most beautiful cities." Tourism is not only a vital pillar of the Western Cape's economy but also a key driver of South Africa's national economic growth.

According to the latest data from Wesgro, South Africa's tourism trade and investment promotion agency, the Western Cape's tourism sector continued its strong recovery trajectory in Q1 2024, with multiple indicators surpassing pre-pandemic levels, demonstrating the province's robust resilience and appeal as a global tourism destination.

Key Highlights: Visitor Growth and Revenue Performance

Significant Increase in Air Passengers

In Q1 2024 (January to March), Cape Town International Airport recorded 336,268 air passengers, a 16% increase compared to the same period in 2023, exceeding 2019 levels. This marks the Western Cape's tourism industry has fully emerged from the pandemic shadow and entered a new growth phase.

Cape Town International Airport's international terminal recorded a 17% year-on-year growth in March, with two-way passengers reaching 318,366; the domestic terminal grew by 11%, with passengers exceeding 640,000. George Airport's passenger volume during the same period recovered to 98% of 2019 levels.

Surge in Attraction Visitors

The 53 participating attractions across six regions in the Western Cape recorded over 2.5 million visitors in Q1, a 23% increase compared to the same period in 2023. The top five attractions were:

1. Table Mountain National Park: 951,767 visitors 2. Table Mountain Aerial Cableway: 346,277 visitors 3. Cape of Good Hope: 299,392 visitors 4. Boulders Beach: 256,098 visitors 5. Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden: 205,207 visitors

Analysis of Major Source Markets

Overseas markets: The UK remains the largest source market, followed by Germany, with the US, Netherlands, and France rounding out the top five. European markets performed particularly well, with eight of the top ten source countries from Europe, indicating full recovery to 2019 levels. Overseas markets that have fully recovered to pre-pandemic levels include: UK, Germany, US, Netherlands, Canada, Belgium, and Ireland. African markets: Namibia leads, followed by Zimbabwe, with Angola, Mozambique, and Kenya in the top five. African continental source markets also showed strong performance, with eight major African markets recovering to 2019 levels: Zimbabwe, Mozambique, Kenya, Mauritius, Nigeria, Botswana, Tanzania, and Ghana.

Analysis of Growth Drivers

Exchange Rate Advantage

The South African Rand (ZAR) maintains weakness relative to major international currencies, providing international tourists with highly attractive travel cost advantages. Calculated in Euros, every 100 international tourists can generate approximately €106,100 in direct tourism consumption for the Western Cape and contribute about €25,260 to GDP.

Infrastructure Improvements

Cape Town International Airport continues to expand and upgrade, adding multiple new international direct flight routes and enhancing passenger reception capacity. Provincial transportation networks, accommodation facilities, and tourism support services are also continuously optimized, providing tourists with more convenient and comfortable travel experiences.

Events and Festivals Driving Growth

The Western Cape hosts numerous internationally renowned events throughout the year, including the Cape Town Marathon, Cape Town Jazz Festival, and Wine Festival, effectively attracting specific visitor segments and extending tourist stays, driving tourism consumption growth.

Government Policy Support

The Western Cape Government launched a R4 million Tourism Growth Fund, aiming to increase visitor numbers, enrich tourism products, and enhance attraction quality, ultimately creating more employment opportunities. The government has set a goal to double visitor numbers by 2035, demonstrating high commitment to tourism development.

Challenges Faced

Seasonal Fluctuations

South Africa's tourism industry exhibits distinct seasonal characteristics. The Western Cape's peak tourism season concentrates during the Southern Hemisphere summer from November to March, with significantly lower visitor numbers during the off-season (April to October), leading to idle tourism facilities and seasonal unemployment. Developing off-season tourism products, attracting business travelers and MICE tourism has become a key issue for sustainable industry development.

Safety Concerns

South Africa's security issues have long affected international visitor confidence. The Western Cape Government has established a Tourism Safety and Support unit, dedicated to handling tourist safety issues and providing emergency support to international visitors. The government has also developed a tourism safety strategy, strengthening attraction patrols, improving transportation facility safety, and establishing cooperation mechanisms with law enforcement.

Visa policies are also a barrier to visitor growth. The South African government is actively simplifying visa application processes, introducing more convenient visa measures for emerging markets like India and China to attract more international tourists.

Contribution to South Africa's Economy

Job Creation

According to World Travel and Tourism Council (WTTC) data, South Africa's tourism industry is expected to support 1.9 million jobs in 2025, a record high, accounting for 11.3% of total national employment. Tourism has become a core industry for labor absorption in South Africa, playing an important role particularly in youth and female employment.

Western Cape data shows that every 100 international tourists can create 2 local job opportunities, making tourism an important pathway for the province to address unemployment.

GDP Contribution

WTTC forecasts that South Africa's tourism industry will contribute R659.8 billion to GDP in 2025, accounting for 8.9% of national GDP. Although still 3.4% below the 2019 peak, it demonstrates a steady recovery trend.

Domestic tourism spending shows even stronger performance, projected to reach R445 billion in 2025, 3.8% above 2019 levels, indicating South Africa's domestic tourism market has fully recovered and continues to grow.

International visitor spending is projected to reach R128.4 billion, though still R37.7 billion below 2019 levels, showing steady growth trends.

Conclusion: Future Outlook

The Western Cape's outstanding tourism performance in Q1 2024 marks a new phase of full recovery for South Africa's tourism industry. Government policy support, infrastructure improvements, exchange rate advantages, and diversified tourism products collectively drive continued tourism growth.

Looking ahead, South Africa will assume the G20 presidency in 2025, providing unique opportunities for tourism development. WTTC forecasts that by 2035, South Africa's tourism industry will add 620,000 jobs, reaching total employment of 2.6 million, accounting for 13.8% of the national workforce; tourism GDP contribution is expected to reach R911.7 billion, accounting for 10.3% of national GDP.

However, seasonal fluctuations, safety concerns, and visa barriers remain major challenges for industry development. Continued investment in tourism safety, visa process simplification, and off-season tourism product development will be key to the Western Cape's sustainable tourism growth.

---

Author

Leo Pan

CEO, Ding Yao International Consultants

South African tourism and economic development professional consultant, with long-term focus on African emerging market economic dynamics and industry development trends. Specialized in tourism strategy planning, market analysis, and investment assessment, helping enterprises seize opportunities in South Africa and African markets.

---

Data sources: Wesgro, Statistics South Africa (StatsSA), Airports Company South Africa (ACSA), World Travel and Tourism Council (WTTC), Western Cape Government
